questions.text: The minimum energy below which no reaction occurs on reactant molecules is the:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Average kinetic energy of the molecule
options.explanation: More or less than the average kinetic energy, a reaction can still take place.
More or less than the potential energy, a reaction can still take place.
A molecule's free energy will not help kick start a reaction.
The minimum energy below which no reaction occurs on reactant molecules is known as the activation energy.

questions.explanation: Activation energy of the molecule = the minimum energy of molecules below which no reaction occurs

options.explanation: This option suggests that the reaction is second-order with respect to both reactants. This is because the exponents are both 1, so it's a first-order reaction with respect to each reactant, and when added together, it becomes a second-order reaction overall.

questions.text: Change in concentration of a reactant is plotted against time and the slope dx/dt determined. The values of dx/dt are plotted against (a-x)2 and a straight line is obtained. It may be concluded that the reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it suggests different order of reaction, which is not consistent with the observed behavior in this scenario.

This option is correct, indicating that the reaction is second order. When the rate of change of concentration with respect to time, dx/dt, is plotted against (a-x)2 (where 'a' is the initial concentration), and a straight line is obtained, it implies that the reaction follows second-order kinetics. This is a characteristic behavior of second-order reactions. In second-order reactions, the rate is directly proportional to the square of the concentration of a single reactant, which leads to a linear relationship when plotted against (a-x)2.
This option is incorrect because it suggests different order of reaction, which is not consistent with the observed behavior in this scenario.
This option is incorrect because it suggests different order of reaction, which is not consistent with the observed behavior in this scenario.

questions.text: Choose the one which is not an assumption of collision theory of reaction rate:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: For chemical reaction to occur molecule / particles must collide
options.explanation: This is a necessary condition for a chemical reaction to occur. If the reactant molecules do not collide, they cannot react.

options.text: For reacting molecules / particles must possess a certain minimum amount of energy, the activation of energy
options.explanation: This is also a necessary condition for a chemical reaction to occur. The activation energy is the energy barrier that the reactant molecules must overcome in order to react. If the reactant molecules do not have enough energy, they cannot react.
Not every collision is productive; only those with sufficient energy and proper orientation lead to a reaction.

options.text: For hydrogen molecule formation from atoms require specific orientation
options.explanation: Collision molecular theory says that for a reaction to occur, reactants must collide in a specific orientation with a specific energy. As hydrogen is a diatomic molecule so it does not require a specific orientation.

questions.explanation: Collision molecular theory says that for a reaction to occur, reactants must collide in a specific orientation with a specific energy. As hydrogen is a diatomic molecule so it does not require a specific orientation.

questions.text: If the reaction A + B -----> AB. If concentration of A and B are doubled, the rate of reaction will:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: According to the solution, this option is not correct.
According to the solution, this option is not correct.

In reaction A + B → AB Rate of forward reaction = k[A][B] According to the condition if the concentration of A and B are doubled, then the rate of forward reaction will increase four times as shown below. Rate of forward reaction = k[A][B] = k [2][2] = 4k
According to the solution, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: In reaction A + B → AB Rate of forward reaction = k[A][B] According to condition if concentration of A and B are doubled, then rate of forward reaction will increases four times as shown below. Rate of forward reaction = k[A][B] = k [2][2] = 4k

questions.text: In which of the following reactions decrease or increase in pressure have no effect on the change of direction of the reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Since the number of moles of reactants = the number of moles of products, so there is no effect of increase or decrease in pressure in the given reversible reaction.

Number of moles of reactants is greater than the moles of the products. If the pressure increases or decreases, the rate of reaction favours the side which will act to reduce the pressure of the system. If the pressure is increased, the rate of reaction moves forward. If the pressure decreases, the backward side is favoured.

Number of moles of products is greater than the moles of the reactants. If the pressure increases or decreases, the rate of reaction favours the side which will act to reduce the pressure of the system. If the pressure is increased, the rate of reaction moves backward. If the pressure decreases, the forward side is favoured.

Number of moles of reactants is greater than the moles of the products. If the pressure increases or decreases, the rate of reaction favours the side which will act to reduce the pressure of the system. If the pressure is increased, the rate of reaction moves forward. If the pressure decreases, the backward side is favoured.

questions.explanation: Option a is correct. Since the number of moles of reactants = the number of moles of products, so there is no effect of increase or decrease in pressure in the given reversible reaction.

questions.text: Which one of the following groups of elements forms strongest bases?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Group IVA elements do not make strong bases.
Group IIIA elements do not make strong bases. Infact, they have amphoteric oxides and hydroxides which can act as both acids and bases.
Group IIA alkaline earth metals have 2 valence shell electrons. They do make strong bases, however, not as strong as IA.

This option is correct because Group IA elements (alkali metals) form the strongest bases. Alkali metals, which include elements like sodium (Na), potassium (K), and cesium (Cs), are highly reactive metals. When they react with water, they form strong bases like hydroxides (e.g., NaOH, KOH). These hydroxides are highly soluble in water and dissociate completely, producing a high concentration of hydroxide ions (OH-) in solution.

questions.explanation: Group IA elements, also known as alkali metals are the most reactive elements as they have only one valence shell electron. They can donate this electron willingly and can make strong bases like NaOH, KOH, CsOH etc. Group IIA make strong bases, but not as strong as group IA. Group IIIA make amphoteric oxides and hydroxides - meaning they can act as both acids and bases. Group IVA do not make bases.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct about the effect of a catalyst?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It increases the equilibrium constant for the forward reaction
options.explanation: A catalyst works by lowering the activation energy required for a reaction to occur. This allows the reaction to proceed more easily and at a faster rate, but it does not change the position of the equilibrium or the equilibrium constant.

options.text: It increases the rate of the equilibrium constant for the forward reaction but not of the reverse reaction
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because a catalyst increases the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions. However, it does not change the equilibrium constant itself.

options.text: It increases the yield of product at equilibrium
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because a catalyst does not directly affect the yield of product at equilibrium. It can speed up the attainment of equilibrium but does not change the position of the equilibrium.

options.text: It provides an alternative route for a reaction
options.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative route for a reaction to take place by lowering the activation energy for the reaction to start and hence, speeds it up.

questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative route for a reaction to take place by lowering the activation energy for the reaction to start and hence, speeds it up.

questions.text: At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are constant. This means that the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in no net change in the overall concentrations of reactants and products.

At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are constant. This means that the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in no net change in the overall concentrations of reactants and products.

At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are constant. This means that the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in no net change in the overall concentrations of reactants and products.

At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are constant. This means that the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in no net change in the overall concentrations of reactants and products.

This means that the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in no net change in the overall concentrations of reactants and products. At dynamic equilibrium:(I) Rate of forward reaction (Rf) Rate of backward reaction (Rb) (II)Concentration of reactant and product becomes constantIt means at equilibrium, concentration of reactant and product does not change, no matter how long the reaction proceeds.

options.text: Both forward and reverse reaction occur simultaneously at the same time under same conditions.

questions.text: The equilibrium expression for a reaction is Ke = X2 / V ( a-X ) , what is true for this reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increase in pressure will favour forward reaction
options.explanation: If volume factor is in denominator of Ke expression, it means moles (volume) of products are more than moles (volume) of reactants. According to Le-Chatelier principle, increase in pressure moves reaction in direction in which volume is less. So, less pressure favours the forward reaction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Decrease in pressure will favour forward reaction
options.explanation: If volume factor is in denominator of Ke expression, it means moles (volume) of products are more than moles (volume) of reactants. According to Le-Chatelier principle, increase in pressure moves reaction in direction in which volume is less. So, less pressure favours the forward reaction.

options.text: Decrease in pressure will favour backward reaction
options.explanation: If volume factor is in denominator of Ke expression, it means moles (volume) of products are more than moles (volume) of reactants. According to Le-Chatelier principle, increase in pressure moves reaction in direction in which volume is less. So, less pressure favours the forward reaction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Increase in volume will favour backward reaction
options.explanation: If volume factor is in denominator of Ke expression, it means moles (volume) of products are more than moles (volume) of reactants. According to Le-Chatelier principle, increase in pressure moves reaction in direction in which volume is less. So, less pressure favours the forward reaction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: If volume factor is in denominator of Ke expression, it means moles (volume) of products are more than moles (volume) of reactants. According to Le-Chatelier principle, increase in pressure moves reaction in direction in which volume is less. So, less pressure favours the forward reaction. Hence, option b is correct.
